skill.name: backend-dev-guidelines
skill.description: Backend development guidance for Node.js, Express, and TypeScript services. It documents layered API architecture, controllers, services, repositories, middleware, validation, configuration, Sentry monitoring, and testing patterns.
skill.summary: Apply consistent Node.js, Express, and TypeScript backend patterns.
skill.author: BrianDai22

security_audit.summary: AI review found no prompt injection, malicious code, or executable skill scripts. Most static findings are false positives from markdown code fences, relative TypeScript imports, local URLs, and environment configuration examples. Medium risk remains because several configuration examples include copy-pastable placeholder secrets and weak development defaults.

medium_findings.title: Copy-Pastable Placeholder Secrets in Configuration Examples
medium_findings.description: Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE for a documentation risk. The configuration guide shows development placeholders such as database passwords, session secrets, JWT secrets, and service tokens; these are not malicious, but users could copy weak values into real deployments.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are explicit configuration examples with secret-like placeholders and development defaults. The surrounding guide also warns not to commit secrets, so this is a copy-paste hardening issue rather than malicious intent.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fences Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE for skill execution risk. Many external command findings come from markdown code fences or documented shell commands, and the skill contains no executable script that runs them automatically.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence is inside markdown examples. I found no code path in the skill that invokes shell execution on behalf of the user.

low_findings.title: Relative Imports Misclassified as Path Traversal
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE for filesystem traversal. The flagged traversal sequences are relative TypeScript imports in examples, not file reads from user-controlled paths.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The snippets are import statements in markdown documentation. They do not perform runtime path resolution or accept user-controlled filesystem input.

low_findings.title: Environment Access Examples Are Documentation, Not Exfiltration
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE for credential theft. The skill discusses process.env and config files to recommend centralized configuration and secret hygiene, with no network exfiltration behavior found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines frame direct env access as an anti-pattern or show .gitignore entries for secret files. No evidence found that the skill reads or transmits environment values.

low_findings.title: Local URLs and Monitoring DSNs Are Example Values
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE for network reconnaissance. The hardcoded URLs are localhost test endpoints or placeholder Sentry DSNs in documentation, not active outbound network calls by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear in examples for configuration and endpoint testing. I found no executable network client or suspicious external destination in the skill files reviewed.

dangerous_patterns.title: Weak Development Secret Defaults in Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The documentation includes placeholder secrets and fallback defaults that should never be copied into production. Marketplace users should treat these as illustrative values only.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples visibly contain weak or placeholder secret values. The risk depends on user copy-paste behavior, not on malicious behavior in the skill itself.

content.user_title: Build Backend APIs With TypeScript Patterns
content.value_statement: Backend services often drift into inconsistent routing, error handling, and configuration patterns. This skill provides practical Node.js, Express, and TypeScript guidance for layered APIs.
actual_capabilities: Explains layered backend architecture with routes, controllers, services, and repositories.
Provides Express controller and route patterns using BaseController conventions.
Documents Zod validation patterns for request bodies and DTOs.
Describes Prisma repository patterns for database access and testing.
Guides Sentry setup for error tracking and performance monitoring.
Covers unified configuration practices and migration away from scattered env reads.
limitations: It is documentation only and does not generate a complete service by itself.
Examples must be adapted to the target repository structure and dependencies.
Placeholder secrets and local URLs must be replaced before production use.
It focuses on Node.js, Express, TypeScript, Prisma, Zod, and Sentry patterns.

use_cases.title: Create a new API endpoint
use_cases.description: Use the route, controller, service, validation, and repository guidance to add an endpoint with consistent structure.

use_cases.title: Refactor legacy route logic
use_cases.description: Move business logic out of large Express route files into controllers, services, and repositories.

use_cases.title: Standardize service conventions
use_cases.description: Use the guidelines to align configuration, Sentry monitoring, async error handling, and test patterns across services.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a backend endpoint
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the backend development guidelines to plan a new Express endpoint for this feature. Include route, controller, service, validation, and test responsibilities.

prompt_templates.title: Review route structure
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this Express route file against the backend guidelines. Identify business logic that should move to controllers, services, or repositories.

prompt_templates.title: Design service and repository layers
prompt_templates.prompt: Design the service and repository structure for this feature using the documented dependency injection and Prisma patterns.

prompt_templates.title: Refactor a legacy backend flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Refactor this legacy backend flow using the guidelines. Preserve behavior while improving validation, error handling, Sentry coverage, configuration, and tests.

output_examples.input: A request to add a user management endpoint.
output_examples.output: A structured implementation plan that separates routing, request handling, business logic, persistence, validation, monitoring, and tests.

output_examples.input: A route file with mixed validation, database access, and response formatting.
output_examples.output: A review that lists responsibilities to extract and recommends controller, service, and repository boundaries.

output_examples.input: A service that reads configuration directly from environment variables.
output_examples.output: Migration guidance that centralizes configuration, validates required settings, and flags placeholder secrets for replacement.

best_practices: Use the skill with the actual repository files open so recommendations match local patterns.
Replace every placeholder secret, token, DSN, and local URL before production deployment.
Ask for tests and monitoring changes when using the skill for implementation work.
anti_patterns: Do not copy example secrets or development defaults into production configuration.
Do not treat documentation examples as complete security reviews.
Do not apply the patterns blindly to non-Express or non-TypeScript services.

faq.question: Does this skill execute code or commands?
faq.answer: No. It is a documentation skill with markdown examples for backend development patterns.

faq.question: Which frameworks does it focus on?
faq.answer: It focuses on Node.js, Express, TypeScript, Prisma, Zod, Sentry, and related backend service patterns.

faq.question: Can it help refactor large route files?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains how to move business logic into controllers, services, and repositories.

faq.question: Are the configuration examples production ready?
faq.answer: No. They contain placeholders and development defaults that must be replaced with secure production values.

faq.question: Does it include testing guidance?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers unit tests, integration tests, mocks, setup, teardown, and authenticated endpoint testing.

faq.question: Is it useful with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The guidance can be used in Claude, Codex, and Claude Code when working on backend repositories.
